text: The surgeon incises the visceral peritoneum over the aorta and continues the incision cranially until reaching the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric artery between clips, completing the separation of this vessel during the abdominoperineal resection.
The surgeon uses an advanced bipolar device to fuse and divide the sigmoid vein, with clips applied for additional protection.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to mobilize the sigmoid colon and lower left colon, avoiding mobilization of the upper left colon due to the nature of the abdominoperineal resection.
The left ureter is visible alongside the ovarian vessels, highlighting their anatomical proximity during the dissection.
The surgeon performs a complete mesorectal dissection around the rectum, carefully preserving the surrounding pelvic nerves within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon completes the posterior, lateral, and anterior mobilization, leaving the anorectal junction attached to the lowermost part of the posterior vaginal wall.
The surgical team proceeds with the abdominoperineal resection via the perineal approach, focusing on the anorectal junction and surrounding structures.
The surgeon separates the anus from the surrounding ischorectal tissue and excises a cuff of the levator ani muscle, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the abdominoperineal resection.
The surgeon delivers the rectum through the perineal wound after dividing the anterior attachment to the vagina, completing the separation of the rectum from surrounding structures.
The surgeon resects the specimen and pushes the proximal colonic stump into the abdomen, exteriorizing it to form an end colostomy.
